title: Grassmann time-evolving matrix product operators for fermionic impurities coupled to a superconducting bath
authors: Chu Guo, Wei Wu, Xiansong Xu, Ping-Xing Chen, Changming Yue, Tian Jiang, Ruofan Chen
abstract: The Grassmann time-evolving matrix product operator (GTEMPO) method, which represents the Feynman-Vernon influence functional as a temporal matrix product state, has been shown to be a flexible and potentially scalable solution for fermionic quantum impurity problems. In this work, we extend GTEMPO to solve fermionic impurity problems in the Nambu formalism, in which the impurity is coupled to a superconducting bath. A key insight is that by employing the Bogoliubov transformation for the superconducting bath, one could obtain the analytic expression of the Feynman-Vernon influence functional in a similar form to the case of a normal bath, after which the core algorithms of GTEMPO can be straightforwardly adapted. We demonstrate the accuracy of our method by benchmarking it against exact diagonalization in several exactly solvable cases, and against the continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo method using converged dynamical mean field theory (DMFT) iterations on the imaginary contour in the non-integrable case. In all cases, we perform both imaginary- and real-time calculations to illustrate the flexibility of our method. These results illustrate that our method could be potentially useful as an impurity solver in DMFT as well as its non-equilibrium extension for fermionic impurity problems in the Nambu formalism.
summary: This paper introduces Nambu-GTEMPO, a numerical method for studying fermionic impurities in superconducting baths. By using a Bogoliubov transformation, the authors map the complex superconducting coupling into a form compatible with existing Grassmann matrix product operator techniques. The method is validated against exact methods and shown to be effective for both equilibrium and non-equilibrium real-time dynamics. Crucially, it avoids the dynamical sign problem that plagues Monte Carlo simulations in the Keldysh regime. This makes it a powerful tool for investigating the physics of the Superconducting Anderson Impurity Model.
main_problem: Extending the Grassmann time-evolving matrix product operator (GTEMPO) method to solve fermionic impurity problems where the impurity is coupled to a superconducting bath, specifically addressing the Nambu formalism.
main_result: The authors successfully developed the Nambu-GTEMPO method, demonstrating its accuracy against exact diagonalization and CTQMC, and showing it can handle real-time non-equilibrium dynamics without the dynamical sign problem.
method: The method uses a Bogoliubov transformation to map the superconducting bath to a normal bath form, representing the Feynman-Vernon influence functional as a Grassmann matrix product state (GMPS).
model_or_system: The Superconducting Anderson Impurity Model (SAIM), featuring a localized fermionic impurity with on-site energy and interaction strength coupled to an s-wave superconducting bath.
key_observables: Imaginary-time and real-time correlation functions (G_up-up, G_up-down, etc.) and impurity state probabilities (vacuum, single, and double occupation).
important_parameters: Interaction strength (U), impurity energy (epsilon_d), superconducting gap (Delta_sc), hybridization function, temperature (beta), bond dimension (chi), and time discretization step (delta_t).
main_assumptions: The bath is assumed to be non-interacting, and the hybridization is linear in terms of bath creation and annihilation operators.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the partial IF algorithm and correlation function calculation; Figure 2 shows imaginary-time correlation functions for a toy model; Figure 4 and 5 show error scaling with respect to time step and bond dimension; Figure 8 shows the time evolution of impurity state probabilities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Nambu-GTEMPO extension, describes the Bogoliubov transformation technique, validates the method using exact diagonalization for simple cases, compares it to CTQMC for interacting cases in DMFT, and performs error analysis and real-time dynamics demonstrations.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems because it provides a sign-problem-free tensor network approach to studying non-equilibrium dynamics in superconducting environments, a regime where traditional Monte Carlo methods fail.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A schematic illustration of the partial IF algo- rithm to build the Feynman-Vernon IF as a GMPS for the SAIM, where the imaginary contour has been used as exam- ple. The green and blue solid circles represent the GVs for the upper and lower spins respectively, the lighter and darker circles with the same color represent a conjugate pair of GVs. Each horizontal line with legs acting on the circles represent a partial IF, where the thicker leg corresponds to the GV shared by all the terms in the partial IF. Thus, the upper and lower horizontal lines mean two partial IFs that correspond to the first and second terms in the product of Eq.(37), respectively. (b) Calculating the four...

FIG. 2. (a,b) The imaginary-time correlation functions G↑↑(τ) (a) and G↑↓(τ) (b) as functions of the imaginary time τ for the toy model. The blue and green dashed lines are the Nambu- GTEMPO results, calculated with χ = 30 and δτ = 0.05, for U = 1 and U = −1 respectively, while the solid lines with the same colors are the corresponding ED results. (c,d) The mean errors of G↑↑(τ) (c) and G↑↓(τ) (d), between Nambu-GTEMPO with χ = 30 and ED, as functions of the imaginary-time step size δτ, where the blue and green dashed lines are results for U = 1 and U = −1 respectively. We have used ε0 = 1, α2 0 = 0.5, β = 5 and εd = −U/2 in these simulations.

FIG. 8. Time evolution of the probability of the four impurity states ∣0⟩(a), ∣↑⟩(b), ∣↓⟩(c) and ∣↑↓⟩(d), for the SAIM. The orange solid lines from lighter to darker are the Nambu- GTEMPO results calculated with different χs from small to large, with fixed δt = 0.05. In the insets, we show the errors between the Nambu-GTEMPO results calculated using δt = 0.05 and δt = 0.05, with fixed χ = 400. We have used U = −1, εd = −U/2 and β = 10 in these simulations.
